meta_title: For Students: SaaS starter kit to Solve can't debug code (Intermediate Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS starter kit and overcome can't debug code. A Intermediate-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: SaaS starter kit to Solve Debugging Frustrations
content.seo_description: Overcome debugging blocks as a student developer. Learn how to use a SaaS starter kit with Antigravity and Claude Code to build faster and ship with confidence.
content.answer_block: Stop fighting syntax errors manually. By leveraging a battle-tested SaaS starter kit, you inherit pre-configured architecture that eliminates common debugging pitfalls. Integrating Antigravity for structural analysis and Claude Code for automated refactoring transforms your workflow, allowing you to focus on product logic rather than fighting your own codebase.
content.intro:
You’re staring at a monitor at 2 AM, heart racing, as a cryptic error message mocks your progress. You’ve spent three hours chasing a ghost in your authentication middleware, and that ambitious SaaS project you promised your portfolio is stalling. The gap between your vision and your execution feels like a canyon, and every 'undefined' error feels like a personal failure.

But here is the secret: professional developers don't have fewer bugs; they have better systems to contain them. By switching to a robust SaaS starter kit, you stop reinventing the wheel and start building on a foundation engineered for success. It’s time to move past the frustration of manual debugging and enter the realm of professional-grade rapid development.

steps.title: Bootstrap with a Production-Grade Starter Kit
steps.description: Initialize a project using a vetted SaaS starter kit to ensure your authentication, database schema, and API routes are pre-configured, reducing the surface area for bugs.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze the current project structure and identify the most common failure points in user authentication flows.

steps.title: Map Your Architecture with Antigravity
steps.description: Visualize your data dependencies to understand how changes in one module propagate errors throughout your app.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a dependency graph for the current repository and highlight circular imports that could cause runtime crashes.

steps.title: Automate Refactoring with Claude Code
steps.description: Use Claude Code to perform bulk refactoring of legacy code blocks into cleaner, more testable modular functions.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Identify code smells in the /lib directory and suggest a refactor strategy for better error handling.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Refactor the error handling in /lib/auth.ts to use custom error classes'

steps.title: Implement Defensive Type Checking
steps.description: Tighten your TypeScript definitions to ensure that null/undefined values are caught at compile-time, not runtime.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Review the current TypeScript configuration and suggest strict settings to minimize runtime exceptions.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Update tsconfig.json to enable strictNullChecks and provide a report on problematic files'

steps.title: Continuous Diagnostic Monitoring
steps.description: Set up automated diagnostic scripts that run on every commit to catch regressions before they reach your local environment.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a diagnostic script that validates API route schemas against the database model.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Create a CI pipeline step that runs a sanity check on all API routes'

pro_tips: Always wrap external API calls in a dedicated 'gateway' pattern to decouple your app from third-party failures.
Use 'console.trace()' instead of 'console.log()' to immediately see the call stack that triggered the error.
When debugging, delete the 'node_modules' folder and lockfile before reinstalling; 30% of weird bugs are just corrupted local caches.
Maintain a 'bug journal' to record exactly what caused your most difficult errors; you will see patterns emerge within weeks.
Apply the 'Smallest Reproducible Unit' rule: isolate the failing logic into a separate file before asking an AI to fix it.
content.next_steps: After completing this setup, deploy your starter kit to Vercel or Railway and begin building your first core feature module using the 'Smallest Reproducible Unit' method.
content.conversion_block: You now have the tools to turn 'can't debug' into 'ship it.' Stop wrestling with broken code and start building the SaaS product that will define your career. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
